data.title: Albania to modernise industrial complex in Elbasan
data.description:
TIRANA (Albania), March 28 (SeeNews) - The government of Albania said on Friday it has prepared a plan for the modernisation of an industrial complex in the central city of Elbasan, which is partially privately owned.

The plan includes infrastructure improvements, assessing the need for more space for existing businesses, and identifying new, innovative businesses to include in the complex, such as communication technology, data centers, and logistics, minister of relations with local governance Arber Mazniku said, as seen in a video posted on Facebook.

Financial details of the project were not revealed.

The complex spans 340 hectares, with 105 hectares now owned by 53 private businesses, primarily manufacturers. According to Mazniku, the yearly turnover of private businesses in the complex amounts to around 1 billion euro ($1.07 billion). Goods produced within the complex contribute 5% of the country’s GDP.

The moderniation of the industrial area will be co-administered by private business and the municipality of Elbasan, according to the Business Improvement District (BID) model, Mazniku explained.

The project is set to be completed by 2030.

data.title: Bernstein Cuts H&M Price Target on 'Weak' Fiscal Q1 Performance
data.description:
Bernstein on Friday reduced its price target for H&M Hennes & Mauritz (HM-B.ST) to 110 kronor from 120 kronor following the Swedish fashion retailer's "weak" fiscal first-quarter results, with misses across the board.

H&M's 2% sales growth at constant currency missed the consensus of 2.2%, and it also reported lower-than-expected gross and operating margins, weighed down by higher markdowns and negative external factors.

"The challenge is the weaker demand environment but it also continues to raise questions about the effectiveness of the brand refresh that started in Q3 last year and the effectiveness of the millions of SEK of marketing spend that have been invested," analysts said. "We think H&M needs to overhaul its design/buying processes, cut its store estate significantly, and manage down its discounting to improve price positioning. Elevating the brand is an appropriate strategy but will take many years to execute and will be difficult without more structural changes."

For the fiscal second quarter, analysts lowered their sales growth outlook to 0.3% on constant currency and reduced their gross margin estimate to 52.8%, 50 basis points below consensus.

The research firm's underperform rating on the stock remains unchanged.

data.title: Ibovespa Slips as Rising Unemployment and Trade War Fears Weigh
data.description:
The Ibovespa fell around 0.2% to below the 133,000 level on Friday, trimming its weekly gains to about 0.4%, as rising unemployment and escalating trade tensions pressured Brazilian equities.

The jobless rate climbed to 6.8% in the three months through February, up from 6.5% in January, signaling early signs of labor market softening.

While still historically low, the uptick reflects a slowdown in private-sector hiring, with employment gains increasingly reliant on public administration jobs.

Meanwhile, trade war concerns intensified after US President Trump announced a 25% tariff on auto imports, alongside broader tariff plans, heightening fears of export disruptions and rising costs for Brazilian firms engaged in global trade.

Heavyweights Ambev, WEG, JBS, and Eletrobras each lost over 1%, leading the declines.

data.title: REG-Annual results for 2024
data.description:
Nasdaq OMX Copenhagen A/S

Charlottenlund, March 28, 2025

Announcement no. 273

Annual results for 2024

The board of directors has adopted the annual report for 2024 today.

  • The group's result of continuing activities before tax amounted to EUR -0.1 million (EUR -4.8 million in 2023). The management considers the result as satisfactory, given the current economic conditions and market conditions in Germany.
  • The group's result of continuing activities before value adjustments and tax amounted to EUR 0.1 million in 2024 (EUR 0.3 million in 2023) which is in accordance with the latest expectations range of EUR 0.1 – 0.3 million, announced in the annual report 2023 as well as in the interim report for the 3rd quarter of 2024.
  • The group's revenue of continuing activities amounted to EUR 4.7 million (EUR 4.5 million in 2023).
  • Negative fair value adjustment of investment properties of the German properties amounted to EUR -0.2 million (EUR -5.7 million in 2023) announced in the stock exchange announcements no. 272 of February 27, 2025.
  • As of December 31, 2024, the total equity of the group amounted to EUR 63.1 million (EUR 57.7 million in 2023), corresponding to an equity ratio of 64.6% (equity ratio of 56.5% in 2023). The equity increased from January 1 to December 31, 2024 primarily due to the capital increase of net EUR 5.9 million, announced in the stock exchange announcements no. 267 of September 24, 2024.
  • The management expects for the fiscal year 2025 a result before value adjustment and tax in the range between EUR 0.3 - 0.7 million.

The general meeting will be held on April 30, 2025.

Any questions can be directed to the undersigned at +45 8110 0800.

Sincerely, German High Street Properties A/S

Hans Thygesen

Chairman of the Board

Attachments

  • Annual Report 2024
  • 529900BT3M81VV58P678-2024-12-31-en

data.title: Ford CEO Took Home Lower Bonus in 2024
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Ford Chief Executive James Farley bonus was lower in 2024, which lowered his total compensation in 2024 from a year ago.

The head of the automaking giant's total compensation in 2024 was $24.9 million, a figure which includes bonuses, stock awards and salary, according to recent filings. In 2023, the executive earned $26.5 million in total compensation.

Last year's lower compensation was due to a lower bonus, or nonequity incentive plan compensation, which came to $1.6 million in 2024, down from $2.4 million a year earlier.

The executive's salary remained flat in the year at $1.7 million, and his stock award was just a little higher at $20.6 million, up from $20.3 million a year earlier.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Abitibi Metals Announces Revised Bought Deal Financing
data.description:
Not for distribution to U.S. news wire services or dissemination in the United States.

LONDON, Ontario, March 28, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Abitibi Metals Corp. (OTCQB: AMQFF) (FSE: FW0) ("Abitibi" or the "Company") has announced today that it has agreed to a revised bought deal financing further to its previous announcement on March 24, 2025. The revised offering is in response to the Province of Quebec 2025 expenditure budget announced on March 25, 2025, which introduced changes to the treatment of Quebec critical minerals flow-through tax credits.

Under the new agreement with a syndicate of underwriters led by BMO Capital Markets, the underwriters have agreed to buy on a bought deal basis 16,071,429 Charity Flow-Through Common Shares (the “Charity Flow-Through Common Shares”), at a price of C$0.45 per Charity Flow-Through Common Share and 8,928,571 Common Shares (the “Common Shares”), at a price of C$0.28 per Common Share, for total gross proceeds of approximately C$10 million (the “Offering”). The Company has granted the Underwriters an option, exercisable at the offering price for a period of 30 days following the closing of the Offering, to purchase up to an additional 15% of the Offering to cover over-allotments, if any. The total issuance of 25,000,000 shares remains unchanged from the previous announcement on March 24, 2025.

Each Charity Flow-Through Common Share will qualify as a "flow-through share" within the meaning of subsection 66(15) of the Income Tax Act (Canada) and, in respect of eligible Québec resident subscribers, section 359.1 of the Taxation Act (Québec).

The Offering is expected to close on or about April 10, 2025 and is subject to Abitibi receiving all necessary regulatory approvals.

The gross proceeds from the Charity Flow-Through Common Shares will be used for continued advancement of the Company’s B26 Polymetallic Deposit and the net proceeds from the Common Shares will be used for general corporate purposes.

The Charity Flow-Through Common Shares and Common Shares to be issued under the Offering will be offered by way of a short form prospectus in all of the provinces of Canada, and may also be offered by way of private placement in the United States.

The securities offered have not been registered under the U.S. Securities Act of 1933, as amended, and may not be offered or sold in the United States absent registration or an applicable exemption from the registration requirements. This press release shall not constitute an offer to sell or the solicitation of an offer to buy nor shall there be any sale of the securities in any jurisdiction in which such offer, solicitation or sale would be unlawful.

About Abitibi Metals Corp.

Abitibi Metals Corp. is a Quebec-focused mineral acquisition and exploration company focused on the development of quality base and precious metal properties that are drill-ready with high-upside and expansion potential. Abitibi's portfolio of strategic properties provides target-rich diversification and includes the option to earn 80% of the high-grade B26 Polymetallic Deposit, which hosts a resource estimate of 11.3MT @ 2.13% Cu Eq (Ind) & 7.2MT @ 2.21% Cu Eq (Inf), and the Beschefer Gold Project, where historical drilling has identified 4 historical intercepts with a metal factor of over 100 g/t gold highlighted by 55.63 g/t gold over 5.57 metres and 13.07 g/t gold over 8.75 metres amongst four modeled zones.

Forward-looking statement:

This news release contains certain statements, which may constitute "forward-looking information" within the meaning of applicable securities laws. Forward-looking information involves statements that are not based on historical information but rather relate to future operations, strategies, financial results or other developments on the B26 Project or otherwise. Forward-looking information is necessarily based upon estimates and assumptions, which are inherently subject to significant business, economic and competitive uncertainties and contingencies, many of which are beyond the Company's control and many of which, regarding future business decisions, are subject to change. These uncertainties and contingencies can affect actual results and could cause actual results to differ materially from those expressed in any forward-looking statements made by or on the Company's behalf. Although Abitibi has attempted to identify important factors that could cause actual actions, events or results to differ materially from those described in forward-looking information, there may be other factors that cause actions, events or results to differ from those anticipated, estimated or intended. All factors should be considered carefully, and readers should not place undue reliance on Abitibi's forward-looking information. Generally, forward-looking information can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as "expects," "estimates," "anticipates," or variations of such words and phrases (including negative and grammatical variations) or statements that certain actions, events or results "may," "could," "might" or "occur. Mineral exploration and development are highly speculative and are characterized by a number of significant inherent risks, which may result in the inability of the Company to successfully develop current or proposed projects for commercial, technical, political, regulatory or financial reasons, or if successfully developed, may not remain economically viable for their mine life owing to any of the foregoing reasons, among others. There is no assurance that the Company will be successful in achieving commercial mineral production and the likelihood of success must be considered in light of the stage of operations.

For more information, please contact Jon Deluce (CEO & President, Director) at 226-271-5170, email info@abitibimetals.com, or visit https://www.abitibimetals.com.

data.title: Natural Gas Edges Down as Mild Weather Cuts Demand and Inventories Rise More Than Expected
data.description:
Natural gas edged lower early on Friday as demand remained weak and inventories rose more than expected.

Gas for May delivery was last seen down US$0.01 to US$3.92 per million British thermal units.

The price of the fuel has traded in a tight range over the past week as the spring shoulder season takes hold, with temperatures too mild to raise heating or cooling demand. Long-term forecasts from the National Weather Service expect states east of the Rocky Mountains to see seasonal or warmer temperatures over the next six to 14 days.

The Energy Information Administration on Thursday reported U.S. natural gas inventories rose by 37-billion cubic feet last week, above the consensus estimate for a 25-bcf rise and leaving stocks at 6.5% under the five-year average.

data.title: Turkish central bank bought 3.8 bln lira in bonds, data shows
data.description:
Turkey's central bank bought 3.8 billion lira ($100 million) worth of TLREF indexed government bond via the quotation method on Friday, central bank data from LSEG showed.

State-owned Anadolu news agency said the aim of the Treasury's and Central Bank's joint efforts was "more effective and healthy functioning of the markets".

The Turkish lira, stocks and bonds have suffered heavy declines since last week when Istanbul mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, the main political rival to President Tayyip Erdogan, was detained. An Istanbul court subsequently remanded him in detention pending trial on graft charges.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0955 ET - Dutch Bros is one of the few restaurants positioned for potential earnings beats in 2025 and 2026, UBS analysts say in a research note. The coffee chain has multiple drivers in place to support sustained sales growth and share gains despite a challenging macro backdrop and tough year-over-year comparisons with a strong 2024, the analysts say. Shifts in its marketing strategy have improved new customer acquisition and driven higher visit frequency from existing customers, they say. The company's loyalty program is incentivizing higher frequency and supporting share gains for some categories, the analysts say. Mobile ordering is also gaining steam and the launch of hot food offerings in 2026 should draw more morning visits, they say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0952 ET - H&M's build-up of inventory tempers expectations around when a change of fortune for the company might come, Deutsche Bank analyst Adam Cochrane writes. The bank sees the significant inventory build as a bigger issue surrounding discounting than management does, but it may be more of a third-quarter question. The company reported a weaker first quarter than expected despite a relatively low bar, but this likely marked the low point in terms of margin decline. March sales growth moved back into small positive territory. Deutsche Bank thinks the stock is getting towards an attractive valuation. The bank lowers its price target to 135 Swedish kronor from 160 kronor and retains its hold rating. Shares fall 1.2% to 131.70 kronor. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0950 ET — Galaxy Digital Holdings expects to make billions on an agreement with CoreWeave, the startup that rents out access to Nvidia chips. The digital asset and blockchain company entered into a 15-year lease agreement with CoreWeave, which makes its public debut later Friday, in which it will provide 133 megawatts of data center capacity to host and support CoreWeave's artificial intelligence and high-performance computing efforts. Over the life of the agreement, Galaxy expects to generate about $4.5 billion in revenue. Benchmark's Mark Palmer in a research note says the estimated revenue from the deal likely reflects CoreWeave's commitment to fund all the capital investments required to upgrade its data centers, among other things. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0936 ET - The winner from President Trump's plans to impose 25% tariffs on imported vehicles? No one, say analysts at Wedbush in a research note. While Trump is pushing for more auto manufacturing in the US, the analysts note that even US automakers that produce domestically have around half of their auto parts come from foreign markets. Tesla, for example, will be affected, as CEO Elon Musk himself acknowledged on his social media platform. "A US car with all US parts made in the US is a fictional tale not even possible today," say the analysts. They believe they will add up to $100 billion of costs annually for the auto industry, something that will be passed to the consumer and erode demand. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0931 ET - Germany's labor market cooled further in March, and the situation could get worse, Deutsche Bank Research economist Marc Schattenberg says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment ticked up to 6.3% from 6.2% in February, while the number of unemployed rose surprisingly sharply by around 26,000 to 2.92 million, he says. Job losses could persist, especially in the manufacturing sector, not least from the repercussions of U.S. trade policy on key German industries, Schattenberg says. Early labor market indicators don't point to any improvement in the coming months, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0930 ET - Oxford Industries missed revenue estimates in 4Q and revealed signs that its business continues to struggle, UBS analysts say in a research note. The company said comparable sales were down 3% in January and then 9% in February, according to the analysts. The accelerating deterioration was attributed to weakening traffic, they say. They believe customers are less willing to shop in the challenging macro environment. Oxford is also guiding for tighter gross margin in fiscal 2025 from tariffs and lower full-price sales. The combination of margin compression and weakening 1Q trends creates downside risk to Oxford's guidance, the analysts say. Oxford Industries sinks 12% in early trading. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0929 ET - Germany's labor market continued its gradual weakening in March as recruitment plans continue to shrink, ING's Carsten Brzeski says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment rose by 26,000, increasing the unemployment rate to 6.3% from 6.2% in February. The unadjusted unemployment number of 2.967 million--albeit a fall from February--is the lowest March figure since the financial crisis, he says. Continued announcements of potential cost-cutting measures in the automotive and other industries are set to push up unemployment further, he says. The latest GfK consumer-confidence reading also published Friday suggests German consumers are focusing more on the cooling labor market than on politics even after elections brought added certainty, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0928 ET - H&M's mild share price reaction yesterday confirmed that investors have mixed feelings about the 33% miss on first quarter EBIT and expectation of a gradual improvement throughout the year, Cedric Rossi at Bryan Garnier writes. Although management guided for a positive discounting impact in the second half of 2024, soft organic growth, rising inventory levels and volatile consumer sentiment pose a lingering threat to the optimistic outlook, he says. Bryan Garnier cuts its FY25-27 estimates by an average of 4%, leading to a lowered price target of 170 Swedish kronor from 175 kronor. It reiterates its neutral rating. Shares fall 1% to 132.05 kronor. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0927 ET - Michelin volumes remain subdued, but EPS is supported by pricing, cost discipline, buybacks, and low financial costs, HSBC analysts write. Following Michelin's February tire market volume data, the bank reduces its 2025 volume estimate by 1%. It expects the first quarter to be the weakest quarter due to tough comparables for light-vehicle tires and still-low demand for truck, construction, and mining tires. Nonetheless, HSBC models a progressive improvement throughout the year. "In our view, continued strength in free cash flow should support higher shareholder returns and be a positive catalyst for the stock." HSBC upgrades the stock to buy from hold and increases its target price to 38 euros from 36 euros. Shares rise 0.6% to 33.04 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0856 ET - H&M is struggling to regain momentum, and the challenging macroeconomic environment and unfavorable purchasing conditions are likely to further complicate its recovery, AlphaValue analyst Jie Zhang writes. The company reported weak first quarter results, with slower top-line growth and profitability missing consensus expectations. March sales are expected to rise by just 1%, highlighting continued demand weakness, Zhang says. Profitability remains under pressure from external headwinds, higher discounting, and ongoing investments in customer offerings. In addition, the U.S. remains H&M's second-largest market by revenue, while the group continues to depend heavily on Asian sourcing. "As such, new U.S. tariffs will inevitably impact H&M's trading." Shares fall 0.6%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0853 ET - Ferrari has presented a clear and swift response to the 25% U.S. auto tariff hike, shouldering some of the burden while also making some price increases, Jefferies analyst James Grzinic writes. The bank estimates Ferrari's direct profit hit this year to be rather immaterial, at around 35 million euros, though the extent to which the balance of the pain will be divided up between dealers and clients is still unclear. "Perhaps the main take-away here is the group's unique ability to provide a very explicit response to such a major, and swift, impact on cost of goods sold." The fact that full-year earnings guidance was reiterated confirms the extent to which contingencies had been built into the outlook, Jefferies adds. Shares rise 3.8%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0839 ET - Oxford Industries reported a solid F4Q that was overshadowed by guidance for the tough year ahead, KeyBanc analysts Ashley Owens and Chris Brazeau say in a research note. The outlook assumes declines in the Johnny Was and Tommy Bahama brands, pressure from tariffs, lower full-price sales and growing overheads from stores and its upcoming distribution center in Georgia, the analysts say. The company's execution has been challenged by consumer distractions and weather-related events, they say, and the unstable macro environment will create even more obstacles in 2025. The analysts downgrade the company to sector weight from overweight. Oxford Industries slides 12% premarket. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: Dutch Bros Among the Few Restaurants Poised for Earnings BeatsMarket Talk
data.description: Dutch Bros is one of the few restaurants positioned for potential earnings beats in 2025 and 2026, UBS analysts say in a research note. The coffee chain has multiple drivers in place to support sustained sales growth and share gains despite a challenging macro backdrop and tough year-over-year comparisons with a strong 2024, the analysts say. Shifts in its marketing strategy have improved new customer acquisition and driven higher visit frequency from existing customers, they say. The company's loyalty program is incentivizing higher frequency and supporting share gains for some categories, the analysts say. Mobile ordering is also gaining steam and the launch of hot food offerings in 2026 should draw more morning visits, they say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: Bulgarian power exchange launching guarantee of origin market
data.description:
SOFIA (Bulgaria), March 28 (SeeNews) - The Independent Bulgarian Electricity Exchange (IBEX), wholly owned by the Bulgarian Stock Exchange, said on Friday that it is launching a guarantees of origin market as part of efforts to support the development of a liberalised electricity market.

The new exchange market will be operated in partnership with Bulgaria's Sustainable Energy Development Agency (SEDA), the IBEX said in a press release.

Trading will take place via an electronic platform allowing participants to initiate purchase and sale auctions with a required collateral. The exchange will facilitate the trade of guarantees of origin issued by SEDA for monthly, quarterly and semi-annual periods.

Registration opens on March 31 for companies registered under the Value Added Tax Act and listed in the SEDA register.

Under the EU directive on the promotion of the use of energy from renewable sources, guarantees of origin certify that a given share or quantity of electricity comes from renewable sources. Member states may also issue such guarantees for energy from non-renewable sources. These certificates have a standard size of 1 MWh.

data.title: SoftBank Group Planning Industrial Parks in U.S., Potentially Promising $1 Trillion in Investments, Nikkei Reports
data.description:
By Denny Jacob

  • SoftBank Group plans to create industrial parks across the U.S. featuring artificial intelligence-equipped factories, Nikkei reports.
  • The Japanese tech investor will potentially promise about $1 trillion in investments to the U.S. government, according to the report.
  • This is a sharp increase from the $500 billion AI infrastructure investment that CEO Masayoshi Son announced back in January.
  • Son is currently believed to be visiting the U.S. and may soon move forward with the mega project, Nikkei reports.

Full story at https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Technology/Artificial-intelligence/SoftBank-Group-plans-1tn-AI-robot-industrial-parks-in-U.S

Write to Denny Jacob at denny.jacob@wsj.com

data.title: Rithm Capital Price Target Maintained With a $14.00/Share by Wedbush
Galaxy Digital Holdings Agrees to 15-Year Lease Agreement with CoreWeaveMarket Talk
data.description: Galaxy Digital Holdings expects to make billions on an agreement with CoreWeave, the startup that rents out access to Nvidia chips. The digital asset and blockchain company entered into a 15-year lease agreement with CoreWeave, which makes its public debut later Friday, in which it will provide 133 megawatts of data center capacity to host and support CoreWeave's artificial intelligence and high-performance computing efforts. Over the life of the agreement, Galaxy expects to generate about $4.5 billion in revenue. Benchmark's Mark Palmer in a research note says the estimated revenue from the deal likely reflects CoreWeave's commitment to fund all the capital investments required to upgrade its data centers, among other things. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: EBRD mulls lending 42 mln euro to Srbija Voz for sleeping cars
data.description:
BELGRADE (Serbia), March 28 (SeeNews) - The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) said on Friday it is considering providing a 42 million euro ($45.4 million) loan to Serbian state-owned passenger railway operator Srbija Voz for the acquisition of 12 railway sleeping cars.

The sleeping cars, suitable for high-speed routes and allowed to operate in the European Union, will support Srbija Voz in expanding its international rail passenger services, the EBRD said in a project disclosure document.

The project also aims to enhance passenger services on existing Srbija Voz routes and to increase overall attractiveness of rail transportation.

The loan is expected to be approved on July 9, the EBRD said.

Srbija Voz was incorporated in 2015 after the unbundling of Serbian state-owned railway operator Zeleznice Srbije.

($ = 0.925 euro)

data.title: Lululemon tumbles as tariff uncertainty, weak demand hit forecasts
data.description:
Lululemon Athletica's shares fell 13% on Friday after the sportswear maker gave downbeat annual forecasts at a time when the broader apparel space battles an uneven consumer demand environment.

The company on Thursday flagged that consumers were spending less due to increased concerns about inflation and the economy.

Lululemon joins a list of retailers rattled by uncertainty around U.S. President Donald Trump's erratic tariff decisions, which have shaken already-weak consumer confidence.

"So the economic conditions are not ideal ... Lululemon has felt some impact from the rising competition in the industry, at least in the United States," Morningstar analyst David Swartz said.

Lululemon has been losing market share to companies such as Alo Yoga and Vuori as it takes longer to rebuild its brand image despite launching a wide array of new clothing.

"Newness initiatives have not been enough to offset pressure in a tough macro," Truist Securities analyst Joseph Civello said.

Some analysts have said there is growing demand for Lululemon's Glow Up tank tops and Daydrift high-rise trousers, but an uncertain environment has dimmed hopes of a rebound.

"We started this year with several compelling new product launches, but we also believe the dynamic macro environment has contributed to a more cautious consumer," CEO Calvin McDonald said during a post-earnings call on Thursday.

At least 12 analysts trimmed their price targets on Lululemon's shares, with Truist being the most bearish cutting by $80 to $380.

The company's shares were trading at $296. They lost roughly a quarter of their value in 2024.

Lululemon's forward price-to-earnings ratio for the next 12 months, a benchmark for valuing stocks, was 21.92, compared with 31.51 for Nike and 25.67 for Adidas , according to LSEG data.

For Lululemon the theme still remains about fading growth, Jefferies analyst Randal Konik said.

data.title: W. R. Berkley jumps after confirming Mitsui Sumitomo intends to purchase 15% of shares
data.description:
** Shares of commercial insurer W.R.Berkley up 14.2% to $75.82

** WRB hits record high and is the highest gainer on S&P 500 index

** Co confirms Mitsui Sumitomo Insurance (MSI) entered into an arrangement with the Berkley family and intends to purchase 15% of WRB's shares in open market or private transactions from third parties

** Berkley family will not be selling any of its common stock to MSI, nor will MSI purchase any shares from WRB

** MSI's investment is anticipated to be completed by end of March 2026

** Including session's move, WRB up 29.6% YTD; it rose 26.5% in 2024

data.title: TD SYNNEX Is Maintained at Buy by UBS
data.description: (13:51 GMT) TD SYNNEX Price Target Cut to $138.00/Share From $150.00 by UBS

data.title: U.S. equity funds see biggest weekly inflows since November
data.description:
U.S. equity funds witnessed robust demand in the week through March 26 as signals of a more measured tariff approach from the Trump administration helped shift investor focus back to prospects of growth in corporate earnings.

Investors acquired U.S. equity funds of a net $22.24 billion, the most for a week since November 13, following $33.53 billion worth of net sales in the prior week, data from LSEG Lipper showed.

However, later in the week, U.S. stocks turned turbulent, after President Donald Trump announced a 25% import tax on foreign-made vehicles and auto parts.

Mark Haefele, chief investment officer at UBS Global Wealth Management, expects near-term volatility to persist but sees U.S. equities outperforming Europe and Asia for the rest of 2025.

"Despite tariff risks, we are confident about U.S. economic prospects and the earnings growth potential for leading AI companies".

Investors poured $23.1 billion into U.S. large-cap equity funds, reversing the prior week's net outflows of $27.38 billion.

Small-cap and multi-cap funds also attracted $3.07 billion and $105 million, respectively, while mid-cap funds posted modest net outflows of $74 million.

U.S. sectoral equity funds, however, saw $1.96 billion in net outflows, led by consumer discretionary funds, which registered the largest weekly disposal in five weeks at $737 million.

Meanwhile, outflows from U.S. bond funds accelerated, with investors pulling $2.97 billion, up from $513 million in the prior week.

Short-to-intermediate government and Treasury funds saw $176 million in redemptions, ending a 13-week streak of inflows.

General domestic taxable fixed income and loan participation funds posted significant outflows of $1.25 billion and $780 million, respectively.

In contrast, money market funds attracted $2.52 billion—their first weekly inflow in three weeks.

At the same time, money market funds received a net $2.52 billion, the first weekly inflow in three weeks.

data.title: TSX Falls as Trade and Growth Concerns Weigh
data.description:
The S&P/TSX Composite Index slipped around 0.3% to below 25,100 on Friday, trimming weekly gains to about 0.3% as investors held back amid uncertainty over impending U.S. tariffs and sluggish economic growth.

President Trump’s announcement of a 25% tariff on foreign-made cars deepened concerns over trade tensions, further fueled by Prime Minister Mark Carney’s warning of potential retaliatory measures.

Meanwhile, preliminary data suggested Canadian GDP stagnated in February, heightening fears of an economic slowdown.

All nine major sectors traded in the red, with most stocks trading flat to negative.

Shopify led the declines, dropping over 2% and extending sharp losses for a third consecutive session.

data.title: HCI Group Says Geico Signs Lease Deal for Florida Campus
data.description:
HCI Group said Friday that Berkshire Hathaway's (, BRB.B) wholly-owned subsidiary Geico has entered into a multiyear lease deal to fully occupy a three-building office campus in Tampa, Florida.

The Tampa campus, which was acquired by HCI's real estate division Greenleaf Capital in 2023, is now fully leased with a new tenant secured, HCI said.
